threats.title: Bribe.withdraw Silent Failure Enables Infinite Reward Capture
threats.hypothesis_description: The Bribe contract's withdraw function silently fails if the requested withdrawal amount exceeds the user's recorded balance. If the Voter contract attempts to withdraw a larger amount than deposited (e.g., due to veNFT balance increases from rebases), the withdrawal is ignored, but the Voter clears the user's vote state. The user can then vote again, inflating their Bribe balance indefinitely and capturing a disproportionate share of the bribe rewards.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a known vulnerability pattern in ve(3,3) forks where weight recalculations cause desyncs between Voter and Bribe accounting. The silent failure in the Bribe contract makes this desync exploitable for infinite reward extraction.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (amount <= balanceOf[tokenId]) { ... } // silent failure if false

preconditions: Voter contract attempts to withdraw an amount greater than the user's Bribe balance (e.g., due to veNFT balance increase)

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: User can repeatedly vote and reset, inflating their balanceOf if withdraw silently fails
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker captures all bribe rewards for the pool by artificially inflating their share of the totalSupply

economic_questions: Does the Voter contract ever pass an amount > balanceOf to Bribe.withdraw?
Can a user increase their veNFT balance between vote and reset?
validation_requirements: Analyze Voter contract's reset() logic
Test vote -> increase veNFT balance -> reset -> vote sequence

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal (enough to get a veNFT and vote)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas costs for voting and resetting
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to 100% of the bribe rewards for the targeted pool
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Multi-block (requires veNFT balance increase between vote and reset)

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Drains bribe rewards from legitimate voters

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Legitimate voters on the targeted gauge
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All bribe tokens deposited into the affected Bribe contract

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify if amount > balanceOf is reachable

threats.title: smNFT Double Counting Causes Underflow, Bricking Protocol Emissions
threats.hypothesis_description: The calculate_rebase function double-counts the smNFT balance when calculating veBlackSupply. Since smNFTs are locked in the ve contract, their balance is already included in _veTotal. Adding _smNFTBalance again causes veBlackSupply to artificially inflate. If the smNFT balance exceeds the circulating supply of BLACK outside the ve contract, veBlackSupply will exceed blackSupply, causing an arithmetic underflow that permanently bricks the update_period function.
threats.investigation_rationale: This logic flaw directly threatens the core emission mechanism of the protocol. If update_period reverts, no new BLACK tokens can be minted, and no gauges will receive emissions, effectively halting the protocol.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint veBlackSupply = _veTotal + _smNFTBalance +_superMassiveBonus; ... uint circulatingBlack = blackSupply - veBlackSupply;

preconditions: smNFTs are minted and locked in the ve contract
The total balance of smNFTs exceeds the circulating supply of BLACK outside the ve contract

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker (or natural protocol growth) locks enough smNFTs to trigger the underflow
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit, but causes catastrophic Denial of Service to the protocol's economic engine

economic_questions: Are smNFT balances included in _black.balanceOf(address(_ve))?
What is the threshold of smNFTs required to trigger the underflow?
validation_requirements: Verify smNFT token custody mechanics
Simulate update_period with large smNFT balances

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Sufficient capital to mint the required threshold of smNFTs
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Single transaction to cross the threshold

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Requires acquiring a large amount of BLACK tokens
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Halts all protocol inflation and gauge rewards

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: The entire future emission schedule of the protocol

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Ratio of smNFTs to circulating BLACK

threats.title: Unbounded Epoch Loop in Bribes.earned Permanently Locks User Rewards
threats.hypothesis_description: The earned() function in the Bribe contract iterates over every epoch since the user last claimed rewards. With the protocol configured for 30-minute epochs (WEEK = 1800), a user who does not claim rewards for just 30 days will force the contract to iterate through 1,440 epochs. The multiple state reads per iteration will quickly exceed the block gas limit, permanently locking the user's bribe rewards.
threats.investigation_rationale: The combination of a short epoch duration (30 minutes) and an unbounded loop over historical epochs creates a guaranteed DoS condition for users who passively accumulate rewards.
deployment_assumptions: WEEK is set to 1800 seconds (30 minutes) as defined in BlackTimeLibrary

proof_of_signal.pattern: for (uint256 i = 0; i < numEpochs; i++) { ... state reads ... }

preconditions: User has unclaimed bribe rewards spanning a large number of epochs
WEEK constant is set to a short duration (e.g., 1800 seconds)

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Natural accumulation of time causes the loop to exceed block gas limits
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit, but causes permanent loss of user funds

economic_questions: How many epochs does it take to hit the block gas limit on Avalanche?
Is there any partial claim mechanism to rescue funds?
validation_requirements: Measure gas cost per iteration of the earned() loop
Calculate the maximum number of claimable epochs before OOG

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Users lose confidence as their rewards become unclaimable

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All unclaimed bribe rewards older than the gas limit threshold

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm lack of partial claim functionality

threats.title: Permissionless Gauge Creation Bloats Arrays, Bricking Epoch Upkeep
threats.hypothesis_description: Anyone can create a gauge for a whitelisted token pair via GaugeManager.createGauge. The created gauge is appended to the unbounded `pools` array. The distributeFees() function iterates over this entire array. An attacker can create hundreds of dust pools and gauges, bloating the array until distributeFees() exceeds the block gas limit. Since EpochController.performUpkeep calls distributeFees(), this will permanently brick automated epoch transitions.
threats.investigation_rationale: Unbounded loops over permissionlessly growable arrays are a classic DoS vector. In this protocol, it breaks the core automation that drives the economic engine.

proof_of_signal.pattern: for (i; i < poolsLength; i++) { address _pool = pools[i]; _distributeFees(_pool); }

preconditions: Attacker has enough capital to deploy dust pools and call createGauge
Tokens used are whitelisted/connectors

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Unbounded for loop over pools array
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker deploys many pairs and gauges to bloat the array
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing attack, or used to extort the protocol by holding epoch transitions hostage

economic_questions: What is the cost to deploy a new pair and gauge?
How many gauges are required to hit the Avalanche block gas limit?
validation_requirements: Calculate gas cost of _distributeFees per pool
Determine the maximum safe size of the pools array

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost of deploying ~100-500 pairs and gauges (gas costs)
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Can be executed over multiple blocks

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Minimal dust liquidity for pair creation
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Halts all protocol emissions and fee distributions

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of expected emissions and fees

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify if paginated distributeFees can bypass the broken performUpkeep

threats.title: BlackGovernor Quorum Manipulation via Current smNFT Supply
threats.hypothesis_description: The BlackGovernor contract calculates proposal quorum using the current total supply of smNFTs (getsmNFTPastTotalSupply() with no arguments) rather than the historical supply at the proposal's snapshot block. An attacker can manipulate the outcome of an active proposal by minting or burning smNFTs to artificially raise or lower the quorum threshold after the proposal has been submitted.
threats.investigation_rationale: Governance systems must use snapshot balances to prevent flash-loan or just-in-time capital manipulation. Using current supply breaks the security model of the voting process.
preconditions: An active governance proposal exists
Attacker has capital to mint smNFTs or existing smNFTs to burn/unlock

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker mints smNFTs to raise quorum above the current 'for' votes, causing the proposal to fail
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker can block unfavorable governance actions (e.g., emission rate decreases) to protect their yield

economic_questions: How much capital is required to meaningfully move the smNFT total supply?
Can smNFTs be minted and burned within the voting period?
validation_requirements: Verify IBlackHoleVotes implementation of getsmNFTPastTotalSupply
Test quorum manipulation during an active vote

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Depends on current smNFT supply and required quorum delta
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect profit by protecting favorable emission rates

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Requires acquiring BLACK tokens to lock
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Right before the voting period ends

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Cost to mint smNFTs vs voting power gained

threats.title: Missing GenesisManager Initialization Bricks Epoch Upkeep
threats.hypothesis_description: The EpochController contract does not set the genesisManager address during initialization. When performUpkeep is called, it attempts to execute genesisManager.checkAtEpochFlip(). Because genesisManager is address(0), this call reverts, completely blocking the automated epoch transition, emission distribution, and fee claiming processes.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a critical configuration gap. If the protocol relies on automation to flip epochs, the missing initialization will cause the automation to fail continuously until an admin manually intervenes.
deployment_assumptions: genesisManager is not set via setGenesisManager before the first epoch flip
threats.config_evidence: V1/upgradeable/deployKeeper.js does not call setGenesisManager after deployment.

proof_of_signal.pattern: genesisManager.checkAtEpochFlip(); // reverts if genesisManager is address(0)

preconditions: EpochController is deployed and initialized
setGenesisManager has not been called by the owner
Epoch condition is met and performUpkeep is triggered

initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit, but causes severe operational failure

economic_questions: Is there a fallback mechanism to distribute rewards if performUpkeep fails?
validation_requirements: Check deployment scripts for setGenesisManager calls
Verify if distributeAll can be called directly by anyone (it can, but performUpkeep is the intended automated path)

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Delays emissions and fee distributions, causing user panic

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm revert on zero address call

threats.title: GaugeManager notifyRewardAmount Zero Weight Token Lock
threats.hypothesis_description: If GaugeManager.notifyRewardAmount is called when the total voting weight across all gauges is zero, the calculated reward ratio is zero. The global reward index is not incremented, but the emission tokens are still transferred from the Minter to the GaugeManager. These tokens become permanently locked in the GaugeManager contract with no recovery mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: This creates a scenario where protocol inflation is minted but permanently lost, breaking the economic conservation invariant of the emission schedule.
preconditions: Minter calls update_period
IVoter(voter).totalWeight() returns 0 (e.g., very first epoch, or all users reset votes)

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Users coordinate to reset votes right before epoch flip to burn protocol emissions
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Deflationary attack: burns circulating supply to increase the value of existing tokens

economic_questions: Can users easily reset votes right before the epoch flip?
Is there any sweep mechanism in GaugeManager?
validation_requirements: Test notifyRewardAmount with totalWeight = 0
Verify lack of recovery functions in GaugeManager

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect profit from reduced token supply

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of one epoch's worth of yield
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: One week's worth of BLACK emissions

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm tokens are transferred before ratio check

threats.title: GaugeManager claimRewards Griefing via Forced Execution
threats.hypothesis_description: The claimRewards function in GaugeManager allows anyone to pass an arbitrary array of NFT IDs and force the GaugeCL contract to distribute rewards to the owners of those NFTs. An attacker can systematically call this function for other users' NFTs, forcing them to claim rewards at suboptimal times (e.g., creating unwanted taxable events, or disrupting compounding strategies).
threats.investigation_rationale: While funds are not stolen, forced execution of financial actions violates user sovereignty over their positions and can cause measurable economic harm through tax implications or strategy disruption.

proof_of_signal.pattern: for (uint256 i = 0; i < _nftIds.length; i++) { IGaugeCL(_gauge).getReward(_nftIds[i], isBonusReward); }

preconditions: Target users have accrued rewards in a CL gauge

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Lack of msg.sender validation against NFT ownership
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker calls claimRewards with victim's NFT IDs
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing attack; no direct profit for the attacker

economic_questions: Does forced claiming disrupt any specific autocompounding vaults built on top of the protocol?
validation_requirements: Verify GaugeCL.getReward sends funds to the owner, not msg.sender
Test calling claimRewards from an unrelated address

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas costs to execute the claims

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: End of tax year or right before a user's planned compounding action
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: User frustration and potential tax liabilities

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Unwanted tax events or lost compounding efficiency

threats.title: GaugeCL notifyRewardAmount Silent Truncation of Large Rewards
threats.hypothesis_description: In GaugeCL.notifyRewardAmount, the uint256 reward amount is explicitly cast to uint128 when calling algebraEternalFarming.addRewards. If the protocol distributes a reward token with a high supply or high decimals where the reward amount exceeds type(uint128).max (~3.4e38), the value will silently truncate. The full amount is transferred from the GaugeManager, but only the truncated amount is added to the farming pool, causing a massive permanent loss of reward tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: Silent truncation is a dangerous pattern when interacting with external protocols that use smaller integer types. It leads to severe accounting desyncs and trapped funds.
preconditions: Reward token amount exceeds type(uint128).max (e.g., meme coins with high supply/decimals)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: uint128(reward) cast without safe bounds checking
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Protocol naturally distributes a large amount of a specific token
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Tokens are permanently trapped in the GaugeCL contract

economic_questions: Are there any whitelisted tokens that could realistically reach this threshold?
Does the protocol plan to support high-supply meme coins as rewards?
validation_requirements: Test notifyRewardAmount with value > type(uint128).max
Verify the difference between transferred amount and added amount

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of expected rewards above the truncation threshold
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: The difference between the actual reward and the truncated uint128 value

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Token decimals and expected reward volumes
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm Solidity 0.8 behavior on explicit downcasting (it truncates silently)

threats.title: BribeFactoryV3 Admin Recovery Calls Wrong Function, Causing Insolvency
threats.hypothesis_description: The recoverERC20AndUpdateData function in BribeFactoryV3 is intended to allow admins to recover tokens while updating the Bribe contract's internal accounting. However, it mistakenly calls IBribe.emergencyRecoverERC20 instead of IBribe.recoverERC20AndUpdateData. This removes the tokens from the Bribe contract without reducing the tokenRewardsPerEpoch mapping. When users subsequently try to claim their earned rewards, the Bribe contract will not have enough balance, causing claims to revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: While this requires admin action, it is a critical logic error in an operational function that directly leads to protocol insolvency and locked user funds.
preconditions: Admin calls recoverERC20AndUpdateData to manage bribe tokens

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Calling emergency function instead of accounting-aware function
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Admin action inadvertently breaks contract solvency
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No profit, causes DoS on user reward claims

economic_questions: How often is this recovery function expected to be used?
validation_requirements: Verify the function call mapping in BribeFactoryV3
Test user claims after recovery is executed

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Breaks trust in the bribe system as users cannot claim rewards

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All remaining bribe tokens in the affected contract

threats.title: Unbounded Referral Fee Allows Admin to Steal 100% of LP Fees
threats.hypothesis_description: The setReferralFee function in GaugeFactoryCL lacks an upper bound check for dibsPercentage. The REFERRAL_FEE_DENOMINATOR is 1000. If a compromised or malicious admin sets the fee to 1000, 100% of the LP trading fees claimed by the gauge will be routed to the dibs address, leaving nothing for the internal bribe (voters). If set > 1000, the fee calculation will underflow, reverting all fee claims and permanently locking LP fees in the Algebra pool.
threats.investigation_rationale: Lack of bounds checking on fee parameters is a severe centralization risk. It allows an admin to instantly rug pull all generated trading fees or DoS the fee distribution system.
preconditions: Admin key is compromised or admin acts maliciously
Admin calls setReferralFee with a value >= 1000

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Admin sets referralFee = 1000 to route all fees to dibs address
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker (admin) extracts 100% of trading fees generated by CL pools

economic_questions: Who controls the dibs address?
Is the admin key secured by a timelock or multisig?
validation_requirements: Test setReferralFee with 1000 and 1001
Verify _claimFees behavior under these conditions

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: 100% of all CL pool trading fees

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Right before a large fee distribution
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete loss of LP fee yield for voters

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All trading fees generated by Algebra CL pools

threats.title: Meaningless Address Validation Allows Bricking Gauge Fee Distribution
threats.hypothesis_description: The setInternalBribe function in GaugeCL attempts to validate the new internal bribe address using `require(_int >= address(0))`. Because addresses are treated as unsigned integers in Solidity, this condition is always true, even for the zero address. If an admin accidentally sets the internal bribe to address(0), subsequent calls to _claimFees will attempt to approve and notify rewards on the zero address, causing the transaction to revert and permanently blocking fee distribution for that gauge.
threats.investigation_rationale: Flawed input validation on critical configuration setters can lead to accidental protocol bricking. While it requires an admin mistake, the lack of safeguards makes it a notable operational risk.
preconditions: Admin calls setInternalBribe with address(0)

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Admin error sets critical contract pointer to zero
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No profit, causes DoS on fee distribution

economic_questions: Can the internal bribe be reset to a valid address after being set to zero?
validation_requirements: Test setting internal bribe to address(0)
Verify _claimFees reverts when internal_bribe is address(0)

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Locks LP fees in the gauge

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: veNFT voters expecting LP fees from the affected gauge
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Trading fees for the specific pool

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm revert on zero address interaction
